race.name: Tour de Cure
race.tagline: America's premier diabetes cycling fundraiser. Multiple editions nationwide. 5 to 100 miles. Red Riders leading the charge. 30+ years of riding to stop diabetes — one pedal stroke at a time.

vitals.location: Multiple locations nationwide, USA (Arizona, Northern California, Chicagoland, New England, Greater New York, Mid-Atlantic, Texas, and more)
vitals.date: Year-round — editions held February through October depending on region
vitals.date_specific: 2026 Arizona: February 14 at Goodyear Ballpark. 2025 Northern California: September 28 at HP Palo Alto. 2025 Chicagoland: August 9 at St. Charles. 2025 New England: August 17 at Lexington.
vitals.field_size: ~6,000+ cyclists total across all editions nationwide. Individual editions range from several hundred to over 1,000 riders.
vitals.registration: Online via tour.diabetes.org. Registration fee typically $10-$30 plus fundraising minimum of $250 ($50 for riders under 18). Some editions have different fee structures.
vitals.entry_fee: $10-$30 registration fee + $250 fundraising minimum ($50 for riders under 18). Some editions have higher fees with fundraising included.
vitals.feed_zones: Fully supported routes with rest stops every 10-20 miles providing water, food, and shade. Bike mechanics on course. SAG vehicles for rider support. Medical support available. Volunteer route marshals at key intersections.
vitals.cutoff_time: Generous. SAG support ensures every rider can complete their chosen distance. This is a ride, not a race.
route_options: Century (100 miles) — available at select editions for experienced cyclists
Metric Century (~62 miles) — the long route at most editions
Family Fun Ride (~5 miles) — family-friendly, minimal elevation

climate.primary: Varies by edition — desert warmth in Arizona (February), summer heat and humidity in the Midwest and Northeast (June-August), mild fall in Texas (October)
climate.description: With editions spanning February through October across the entire United States, Tour de Cure climate conditions depend entirely on which edition you ride. The Arizona edition in February offers mild desert mornings (50-65F) warming to comfortable afternoons (70-80F) with minimal humidity and abundant sunshine. Chicagoland in August means Midwest summer heat (75-90F) with humidity. New England in August delivers similar heat with coastal breeze for some routes. Northern California in September offers classic Bay Area conditions — morning fog burning off to warm, dry afternoons (65-80F). Texas in October can still deliver heat (80-90F) with some humidity.
challenges: Summer editions (Chicagoland, New England, Greater New York) can deliver significant heat and humidity
Arizona edition in February has low humidity but direct sun exposure in open desert terrain
Sun exposure across all editions — routes are generally on open roads with limited shade
Afternoon wind at many locations, particularly in coastal and desert editions
Variable conditions within a single ride — morning chill giving way to midday heat

terrain.primary: Paved roads — flat to gently rolling, designed for mass participation across all ability levels
terrain.surface: All paved roads. Course profiles vary by edition but are universally designed to be accessible. The Arizona edition routes through the Estrella foothills with a mix of flat stretches and gentle rolling terrain — the 62-mile route includes approximately 1,289 feet of elevation and a lap around the Phoenix Raceway NASCAR track. Chicagoland routes through the Fox Valley on rolling Illinois farmland. New England courses traverse rolling Massachusetts countryside. Northern California winds through Silicon Valley and Peninsula roads. All routes are well-marked with course marshals at intersections.
features: All-abilities course design — every edition offers routes from 5 miles to 62-100 miles
Arizona: Estrella foothills and a unique lap around Phoenix Raceway (NASCAR track)
Northern California: Silicon Valley and Peninsula roads through Woodside and Portola Valley
Chicagoland: Fox Valley rolling farmland and the Fox River Trail
New England: Massachusetts countryside roads from Lexington
Fully marked courses with volunteer marshals, rest stops, and SAG vehicles

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 54. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 56.

course_description.character: Tour de Cure courses are designed for mass participation, not athletic challenge. Every edition offers multiple distance options from 5 to 100 miles, all on paved roads with gentle-to-flat terrain. The Arizona edition is representative: starting from Goodyear Ballpark, riders head into the Estrella foothills on smooth desert roads with long sightlines and minimal traffic, then take a unique lap around the Phoenix Raceway NASCAR track before returning to the ballpark for the festival. The Northern California edition winds through the oak-dotted hills and quiet roads of Woodside and Portola Valley — some of the most scenic cycling in the Bay Area. Chicagoland routes follow the Fox River Valley through Illinois farmland. New England courses traverse Massachusetts countryside from Lexington. The terrain is universally forgiving — these are rides designed so that a first-time cyclist on the 5-mile family route and a century rider on the 100-mile route can both finish with smiles, finisher medals, and the knowledge that their effort matters.
course_description.signature_challenge: Tour de Cure's signature challenge is not a climb or a headwind — it is the decision to ride. To register, raise $250, show up, and pedal alongside people who live with diabetes every day. The physical effort is modest by design. The emotional weight of riding for 37 million Americans with diabetes, riding alongside Red Riders who are fighting the disease while they ride — that is the challenge that matters.

logistics.airport: Varies by edition. Arizona: Phoenix Sky Harbor (PHX), 25 miles to Goodyear Ballpark. Northern California: San Francisco (SFO) or San Jose (SJC), both within 30 miles of Palo Alto. Chicagoland: Chicago O'Hare (ORD) or Midway (MDW), ~45 miles to St. Charles. New England: Boston Logan (BOS), 15 miles to Lexington. Most participants drive to their local edition.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Single-day events — no overnight required. Most participants drive from home to the start venue. For out-of-town riders, book a hotel near the start location the night before. Arizona: Goodyear/Litchfield Park hotels. Northern California: Palo Alto/Mountain View hotels. Chicagoland: St. Charles/Geneva hotels. New England: Lexington/Burlington hotels.
logistics.food: Rest stops every 10-20 miles with water, electrolyte drinks, fruit, and snacks. Post-ride festival includes a complimentary catered lunch. Beer garden for adults. Vendors and food trucks at many editions. You will not go hungry.

history.origin_story: The American Diabetes Association launched Tour de Cure in 1991 as its premier cycling fundraising event, bringing the cycling community together to raise money and awareness for diabetes research, prevention, and education. The concept was simple and powerful: ride your bike, raise money, fight diabetes. From a handful of regional events, Tour de Cure grew into a nationwide series held across 44 states at its peak. In 2007, the Red Rider program was created by Mari Ruddy, a rider in the Colorado Tour de Cure, to recognize and celebrate participants living with type 1 or type 2 diabetes — transforming them from riders into symbols of courage and resilience. By 2011, the event reached its peak with more than 55,000 cyclists participating in 80 events, raising over $18 million in a single year. Over three decades, Tour de Cure has cumulatively raised hundreds of millions of dollars for the American Diabetes Association's mission. The event series has consolidated in recent years, with fewer but more focused regional editions, but the core mission remains unchanged: get on your bike and ride to stop diabetes.
notable_moments: 1991: American Diabetes Association launches Tour de Cure as its premier cycling fundraiser
2007: Red Rider program created by Mari Ruddy — riders with diabetes recognized and celebrated
2007: Tour de Cure raises over $13 million for diabetes research
2008: 38,000 cyclists in 78 events raise nearly $16 million
2011: Peak year — 55,000+ cyclists in 80 events raise $18 million+
2020s: Event series consolidates to fewer, more focused regional editions
history.reputation: America's most accessible charity cycling event. Where the cycling community rides to stop diabetes — one pedal stroke, one dollar, one Red Rider at a time.

biased_opinion.summary: Tour de Cure is what happens when the American Diabetes Association hands the cycling community a simple assignment: ride your bike, raise some money, and help 37 million Americans who live with diabetes every single day. Since 1991, tens of thousands of cyclists have answered that call across dozens of editions nationwide, raising hundreds of millions of dollars for research, prevention, and education. This is not a race. This is not an endurance test. This is cycling at its most democratic — a 5-mile family fun ride and a 100-mile century starting from the same parking lot, united by the same cause. The Red Rider program gives riders living with diabetes the center stage they deserve, wearing distinctive jerseys that say: I have this disease, and I am still here, still riding, still fighting. The routes are flat, the distances are manageable, and the post-ride festival is a genuine celebration. Tour de Cure will never make a 'hardest rides' list. It was never trying to. It makes the list that matters — the one where cycling does something good for someone other than the rider.
strengths: Accessibility: Routes from 5 to 100 miles at every edition. Fundraising minimum of $250 is within reach. No cycling experience required for the shortest distances. This is the most accessible major charity ride in America.

The Red Rider program: Riders living with type 1 or type 2 diabetes are recognized, celebrated, and centered. Riding alongside someone who manages their disease while raising money to fight it is powerful.
Nationwide reach: With editions across the country — Arizona, Northern California, Chicagoland, New England, Greater New York, Mid-Atlantic, Texas, and more — there is likely a Tour de Cure near you.
The post-ride festival: Finisher medals, catered lunch, beer garden, live music, health and wellness village, farmers market. The celebration matches the mission.
weaknesses: Not challenging: The courses are designed for mass participation, which means experienced cyclists may find the routes too easy. The century options exist but are not the event's focus.
Franchise variability: With multiple editions, quality and atmosphere can vary. Some editions are large and vibrant; others are smaller and less polished.
Scale has contracted: From a peak of 55,000 cyclists in 80 events (2011) to approximately 6,000 cyclists in fewer editions. The event series has consolidated significantly since its peak.
No competitive element: Zero timing, zero results. If you want to test yourself against a course or a clock, this is not the event.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Tour de Cure is the charity ride for everyone. Low barrier to entry, strong organizational support, meaningful cause, and a post-ride party that makes the effort worth it. It will not push your limits as a cyclist, but it will remind you why cycling matters beyond Strava segments and FTP tests.

final_verdict.one_liner: America's most accessible charity cycling event. Flat roads, big hearts, 30+ years of riding to stop diabetes.
final_verdict.should_you_race: You do not race Tour de Cure — you ride it with purpose. Yes if you want a well-organized, fully supported charity ride with a low barrier to entry ($250 fundraising minimum) and routes from 5 to 100 miles. Yes if you want to ride alongside Red Riders who live with diabetes and channel your cycling into something meaningful. Yes if you want a post-ride festival with food, music, and community. Skip if you need challenging terrain, competitive timing, or a true endurance test — Tour de Cure is designed for participation, not performance.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a more challenging charity ride with deeper emotional immersion: Pan-Mass Challenge (177 miles, $6,000 fundraising minimum, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute). If you want a large-scale single-day charity ride with more terrain: Pelotonia (25-200 miles, Ohio, cancer research). If you want a massive community ride with no fundraising requirement: El Tour de Tucson (102 miles, Arizona). If you want the biggest single-day charity ride experience: Five Boro Bike Tour (40 miles through all five NYC boroughs, 32,000 riders).

videos.title: Big Rick Tour de Cure
videos.description:
Big Rick Tour de Cure

The 2013 Silicon Valley Tour de Cure held at the HP campus in Palo Alto California. There were over 2000 riders and they raised over $1 million!

The Tour de Cure is a series of fundraising cycling events held in 44 states nationwide to benefit the American Diabetes Association.

The Tour is a ride, not a race, with routes designed for everyone from the occasional rider to the experienced cyclist. Whether you ride 10 miles or 100 miles, you will travel a route supported from start to finish with rest stops, food to fuel the journey and fans to cheer you on!

WHY WE RIDE

Here are some of the reasons why Tour de Cure riders say they ride:

"I RIDE FOR MY MOM WHO HAS DIABETES."

"I RIDE FOR MY DAD WHO DIED FROM DIABETES."

"I RIDE IN HONOR OF A FRIEND THAT LIVES EACH DAY WITH THIS DAUNTING DISEASE."

"I RIDE FOR MY SON WHO HAS TYPE 1 DIABETES."

"I RIDE FOR MYSELF!"

"I RIDE TO SUPPORT A WORTHY CAUSE!"

"I RIDE FOR THOSE WITH TYPE 2 DIABETES WHO ARE RIDING THEIR BIKES TO STAY HEALTHY!"

Why will you ride this year? What's your reason?

Many people will participate in the Tour de Cure for the stimulating competition, camaraderie and physical cycling challenge, but the real reward comes in knowing that every mile we ride and every dollar we raise brings us that much closer to stopping this disease that affects over 26 million Americans.

http://tour.diabetes.org/
http://www.facebook.com/adatourdecure
http://twitter.com/AmDiabetesAssn
@AmDiabetesAssn

videos.title: Why the Tour de France is so brutal
videos.description:
It's all about the climbs.

Subscribe to our channel! http://goo.gl/0bsAjO

The Tour de France is the most prominent cycling race in the world. It’s now in its 109th edition and it’s being called the ‘Highest in History’ because there are more climbs than ever before. This is important because the climbs are where the race is won and lost. The best riders rely on their teammates to get them through the long, flat, and hilly stages, before they take on the mountain stages on their own. These are the hardest and most brutal stages of the race - but they are exactly what makes the Tour de France famous. 

Vox.com is a news website that helps you cut through the noise and understand what's really driving the events in the headlines. Check out http://www.vox.com.

Watch our full video catalog: http://goo.gl/IZONyE
Follow Vox on Facebook: http://goo.gl/U2g06o
Or Twitter: http://goo.gl/XFrZ5H
videos.transcript:
[Music]
if you look closely at this pack of
riders in the Tour de France you'll see
some of the best cyclists in the world
there here in the back they're keeping
up but also resting while their
teammates in the front do most of the
work it's a technique called drafting

three-week race around France but

on these parts of the route the long
flat and hilly stages eventually these
riders reach stages where they won't be

climbs they're the most brutal and
exciting parts of the race and they're
what makes the Tour de France the most
famous bike ride in the world
the Tour de France began as a desperate

it went on to become such a success that
Lauda made it an annual event year after
year they added new routes to make the

interesting for people to follow by 1908
lottos sales had more than doubled then
in 1910 one of dis ground riders
Alphonse 10a suggested adding a new
twist to the route the tormal a it was a
brutal nineteen kilometer uphill climb
ascending fourteen hundred meters to the
summit to see if this was even possible
stenz jumped in his car to make the
climb he drove up but his car got stuck
in the snow at the top
he suffered from hypothermia and nearly
died but nonetheless he sent a telegram
saying normal a crossed very good road
perfectly passable so the tormal a made
his debut in the 1910 Tour French
cyclist octave Louise was the first to
make the climb successfully but he had
to walk up some parts and famously
called officials assassins when he
reached the summit so he went on to win
the whole tour and his statue was placed
at the top of the tour Millay since then
climbs became a staple of the Tour de
France this year the route is made up of
21 stages over 23 days it features 30
major climbs seven of them are in the
tour's most difficult category
including the tormal a which is part of
the event for the 86th time more than
any other climb in the tourist history
these climbs are where the tour is
ultimately won or lost these are the
long flat and hilly stages that are
usually about 200 kilometers long
here cyclists ride together in a
formation called a peloton it allows
cyclists to save energy by sitting
behind another rider or drafting at high
speeds riders use most of their energy
to pedal against wind resistance but
when a rider stays close behind another
they're sheltered from much of that so
pedaling becomes much easier and they
can keep up with the front riders the
way to measure this is to look at how
much power a cyclist generates here at
the front of the peloton a Tour de
France rider will generate at least 300
watts of power I jumped on a bike to see
what that feels like and just two
kilometers holding 300 watts was really
really hard by comparison when a rider
is behind the lead in the peloton they
only need to generate about 240 watts to
move at the same speed holding 240 watts
for two kilometers felt remarkably
easier so even though these two might
finish a 200 kilometer flat stage at the
same time one is going to be way less
tired than the other that's why you see
some of the tour's best riders here in
the back they're drafting up their
teammates whose job it is to do the hard
work now so that the team's best cyclist
is rested for the most difficult part
the mountains where they'll have to be
on their own
when the peloton starts pedaling uphill
it slows down at this stage the race is
less about fighting wind resistance and
more about gravity which affects all
riders the same way in the peloton so
now every rider in the front and the
back needs to generate an extraordinary
amount of power to keep up pace in 2010
for instance Danish cyclist Chris anchor
Sorenson made it to the front of the
pack on the tormal a climb look at the
facial bruising consolidation out the
pain at the front end as the lead he
dictated the pace of the whole group
this chart shows his power output on the
final climb he averaged 415 watts for
more than 11 minutes face of Chris Anka
Sorensen now really dishing out the
paper and around here he peaked at an
incredible 590 once to mafic riding by
Chris a Kasasa but how long can he keep
this up now look at American cyclist
Chris Horner even though he was several
positions behind Sorenson his power
output was almost the same the faster
Sorenson climbed the harder it was for
the rest of the peloton to keep up and
so the formation started to break up as
weaker riders fell behind this is the
moment in the race when the tour's best
riders switch from drafting to relying
on their own strength to get ahead the
race is breaking up behind them like two
of the best riders here Luxembourgian
Andy Schleck and Spaniard Alberto
Contador who were coasting in the back
of the peloton during the long flat
stages but here they are halfway up the
tormal a Breaking Away for the win each
likely generating well over 400 watts
for the final eight kilometers
Contador in the yellow jersey was the
overall Tour de France leader but only
by eight seconds in second place was
Schleck who would try and lose him on
this climb it was neck-and-neck to the
very top
SiC on the right Contador slack wins
Contador takes second slick edge
Contador by a hair at the top of the
tormal aide to win the stage but since
he didn't lose him
Contador kept his overall lead and went
on to win the Tour de France this kind
of drama is only possible in the
mountains and this year's route makes
climbs particularly important this
year's Tour is being called the highest
in history because of how many climbs
there are over the course of three weeks
just one day features seven climbs even
after more than two weeks in the race
riders climbed a two thousand seven
hundred and seventy meters above sea
level where the thin air makes climbing
even harder that's what makes the Tour
de France the most grueling and
prestigious race in this sport the
winner isn't simply the strongest rider
but the one who endures the most pain
and ultimately has what it takes to
conquer the mountains

videos.title: The Tour 21: Riding for Survival 2023, Episode 1 | Cycling on NBC Sports
videos.description:
Through The Tour 21, a team of 25 amateur cyclists ride the full grueling Tour de France route and traverse over 3,500km in just 21 days to raise money for Cure Leukaemia.

To donate to the team taking on the challenge, visit https://www.justgiving.com/campaign/thetour21in2023

For more information on riding the full Tour de France route in 2023, visit https://www.thetour21.com

#TourdeFrance #Cycling #CureLeukemia 
» Subscribe to NBC Sports: https://www.youtube.com/nbcsports?sub_confirmation=1
» Watch Live Sports on NBCSports.com: http://www.nbcsports.com/live
» Get more cycling news on NBC Sports: https://www.nbcsports.com/cycling

NBC Sports Group serves sports fans 24/7 with premier live events, insightful studio shows, and compelling original programming. NBC Sports is an established leader in the sports media landscape with an unparalleled collection of sports properties that include the Olympics, NFL, Premier League, NHL, NASCAR, PGA TOUR, the Kentucky Derby, Tour de France, French Open, IndyCar and many more. Subscribe to our channel for the latest sporting news and highlights!

With extensive coverage on NBC Sports and NBC Sports Network, the NBC Sports Group is committed to cycling. Covering two of the three world-famous Grand Tours, La Vuelta a España (NBCSN) and cycling’s most prestigious race, Le Tour de France (NBC & NBCSN), NBC Sports features more live, multi-platform and surrounding coverage of premier cycling events than any other network.

Spanning nine months of the year, NBC Sports cycling is anchored by the Tour de France. NBC Sports delivers 23 days of live coverage plus an enhanced primetime show for each of the stages. All stages are also live streamed on a dedicated app, NBC Sports Gold. NBCSN features live coverage of some of America’s biggest cycling events including the AMGEN Tour of California and the Critérium du Dauphiné, in addition to the Paris Roubaix, Liège-Bastogne-Liège and, one of the most important stage races, the Paris-Nice.

videos.title: The Tour 21: Riding for Survival 2022, Episode 5 | Cycling on NBC Sports
videos.description:
The Tour de France is one of cycling's most grueling races, but this year a team of 18 amateur riders will attempt to take the same route -- over 3,500 km -- to raise money for Cure Leukaemia, a British charity aiming to help blood cancer patients. 

To donate to the team taking on the challenge, visit https://www.justgiving.com/campaign/thetour21

For more information on riding the full Tour de France route in 2023, visit https://www.thetour21.com

rider_intel.search_text: The provided transcripts do not contain information about the Tour de Cure gravel cycling race. Instead, they contain content about the Tour de France professional cycling race and Tour 21, a charity cycling event that follows the Tour de France route. The transcripts discuss Tour de France climbing stages, drafting techniques in professional cycling, and a multi-week charity ride through France's mountain ranges to raise money for leukemia research. No specific details about Tour de Cure's gravel course conditions, terrain, challenges, or race experience are mentioned in these transcripts.
